structured_choices.data_source: Electronic health records (EHR) from a single large hospital system, spanning 5 years (60 months). All adult patients (≥18 years) with a primary or secondary diagnosis of heart failure (ICD-10: I50.x) at discharge. Approximately 20,000 patient records with complete clinical notes, imaging studies (echocardiograms), structured demographics, comorbidities, medications, and readmission status within 30 days.
structured_choices.label_definition: Binary outcome: readmission to any acute care hospital within 30 days of discharge (yes/no). Readmission is defined as admission to the same hospital system or any other hospital, verified through EHR and claims data where available. The 30-day window is measured from discharge date to readmission date. Patients who died within 30 days are excluded from analysis to avoid conflating mortality with readmission.
structured_choices.split_method: Temporal stratified split with patient-level integrity. The 60-month dataset is divided chronologically: months 1–36 for training (36 months), months 37–48 for validation (12 months), and months 49–60 for test (12 months). Within each temporal window, stratification is applied by readmission outcome to preserve class balance across splits. Rationale: temporal split prevents future information leakage (models cannot learn from outcomes they should not know during deployment); stratification ensures each split has equivalent readmission rates and prevents training on imbalanced subsamples. Patient-level integrity ensures no patient appears across splits—if a patient has multiple admissions in the dataset, all admissions for that patient are assigned to a single split to prevent data leakage and ensure independent evaluation.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing transformers (NLP tokenizers, image normalization parameters, numerical scalers, categorical encoders, imputation statistics) are fit exclusively on the training set (months 1–36). These fitted transformers are then applied identically to validation and test sets without re-fitting. Clinical note text is tokenized and vectorized using a pretrained biomedical language model (e.g., BioBERT or SciBERT) fit on general biomedical corpora; this model is not fine-tuned on training labels to avoid introducing label information into the feature space. Imaging data (echocardiograms) is normalized using mean and standard deviation computed from training images only. Missing values in structured data are imputed using training-set statistics (median for continuous, mode for categorical); no information from validation or test sets influences imputation parameters.
structured_choices.features: Two feature groups are constructed: (1) Clinical notes features—derived from unstructured discharge summaries and clinical documentation using a frozen pretrained biomedical transformer model (BioBERT or equivalent), generating 768-dimensional contextual embeddings per note; no label-correlated fine-tuning is performed. (2) Imaging features—from echocardiogram images, extracted via a pretrained convolutional neural network (e.g., DenseNet-121 pretrained on ImageNet), generating 1024-dimensional feature vectors; no label-driven fine-tuning on imaging. (3) Structured demographic and clinical features—age, sex, ejection fraction, BNP level, comorbidity count (Charlson index components), current medications (ACE inhibitors, beta-blockers, diuretics, statins), admission source, length of stay, and reason for admission (categorized). All structured features are standardized (z-score scaling for continuous, one-hot encoding for categorical) using training set statistics. No engineered features are constructed that could encode the readmission label (e.g., no post-discharge adverse events, no readmission-predictive billing codes). Feature engineering is completed before any model training begins.
structured_choices.model: Multi-modal deep learning architecture: (a) Text encoder branch: frozen BioBERT embedding layer + 1 bidirectional LSTM layer (256 units) + attention pooling; (b) Image encoder branch: frozen DenseNet-121 + global average pooling; (c) Structured data branch: dense layers (128 → 64 units, ReLU activation). Three branches are concatenated (1024 + 256 + 64 = 1344 dimensions) and fed through a shared dense network (2 hidden layers: 256 and 128 units, ReLU, 20% dropout per layer) to a sigmoid output (binary classification). Model is trained with Adam optimizer (learning rate 1e-4), binary cross-entropy loss, and early stopping on validation loss (patience=10 epochs). Maximum 100 epochs, batch size 32.
structured_choices.baseline: Unimodal baseline using clinical notes only. Architecture: frozen BioBERT embeddings + 1 bidirectional LSTM + attention pooling + dense layers (128 → 64 units) → sigmoid output. Identical optimization, loss function, and early stopping criteria as the multi-modal model. Tuning effort is equivalent: both models undergo hyperparameter search (learning rate ∈ {1e-5, 1e-4, 5e-4}, LSTM units ∈ {128, 256, 512}, dropout ∈ {0.1, 0.2}) via random search over 20 trials on validation data. The baseline uses the same pretrained biomedical transformer (not fine-tuned) to ensure feature quality is not a confound. Rationale: this baseline isolates the contribution of imaging and structured data while controlling for model capacity and training procedures; it answers whether multi-modal fusion provides benefit beyond text alone.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Area Under the Precision-Recall Curve (AUPRC). Justification: readmission is a minority class event (typical hospital readmission rates are 15–25%), making AUROC misleading due to high true negative rates. AUPRC directly measures the model's ability to identify readmission cases among all positive predictions and is sensitive to class imbalance. From a clinical operational perspective, hospitals care about precision (avoiding false alarms that waste resources) and recall (not missing high-risk patients); AUPRC balances both. The test set AUPRC is the primary basis for model selection and hypothesis validation.
secondary_metrics: Sensitivity (recall) at fixed specificity (e.g., 90% specificity) — clinically actionable threshold for flagging patients for intervention
Positive Predictive Value (PPV) — hospital resource planning (% of flagged patients who actually readmit)
AUROC — for comparison to published readmission prediction models
F1-score at optimized decision threshold — general classification balance
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Hyperparameter tuning (grid/random search over 20 trials) uses validation data (months 37–48) only. The trial with the highest validation AUPRC is selected. This model's weights are then fixed, and performance is evaluated on the held-out test set (months 49–60) exactly once. No further tuning or threshold optimization occurs on test data. The test set provides the sole estimate of generalization performance.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set is never used during development, tuning, or model selection. It is held entirely out-of-sample until final evaluation. After model selection is complete (based on validation AUPRC), the selected multi-modal model and baseline model are each evaluated on the test set with all metrics computed. Predictions and metrics are recorded. No retraining, retuning, or threshold adjustment is permitted after test evaluation begins. Test results are finalized in a single pass.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Confounds are addressed via stratification and subgroup analysis: (1) Demographic stratification — test set performance is reported stratified by age group (18–65, 65+), sex, and race/ethnicity to detect model bias. (2) Comorbidity stratification — performance is evaluated separately for patients with 0–1, 2–3, and 4+ comorbidities (Charlson index) to ensure multi-modal gains are not artifacts of treating easier subgroups. (3) Treatment stratification — test set is divided by medication class (patients on ACE inhibitors vs. not; on diuretics vs. not) to check that model improvements are not driven by treatment selection biases. (4) Temporal stratification — test set is split into early (months 49–54) and late (months 55–60) to detect concept drift or seasonal effects. (5) Documentation quality check — patient records are categorized by note length and imaging availability; performance is reported for records with complete vs. partial imaging to quantify the imaging contribution. (6) Exclusion of mortality: patients who died within 30 days are excluded entirely to ensure the label reflects true readmission, not survival bias. Expected claims: the multi-modal model will achieve higher AUPRC than the text-only baseline on the held-out test set and across all subgroups listed above, demonstrating that imaging and structured data contribute generalizable signal, not noise or confound-driven artifacts.

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: Multi-Modal Deep Learning for 30-Day Readmission Prediction in Heart Failure

### 1. Data Collection and Definition

The experiment uses 5 years of electronic health records from a single large hospital system. All adult patients (≥18 years) with a heart failure diagnosis (ICD-10: I50.x) at discharge are included. The dataset contains approximately 20,000 patient records with discharge summaries (unstructured clinical notes), echocardiogram images, structured demographic and clinical variables (age, sex, ejection fraction, BNP, comorbidities, medications, length of stay), and a binary readmission outcome.

The outcome is defined as readmission to any acute care hospital within 30 days of discharge. Readmission is verified through EHR records and claims data. Patients who died within 30 days are excluded to isolate readmission from mortality effects.

### 2. Data Split Strategy

A temporal stratified split is used to prevent information leakage and reflect deployment reality:
- **Training set:** months 1–36 (36 months of historical data)
- **Validation set:** months 37–48 (12 months for model selection)
- **Test set:** months 49–60 (12 months for final evaluation, never touched until the end)

Within each temporal window, stratification by readmission outcome ensures balanced class representation. Patient-level integrity is enforced: if a patient has multiple admissions, all admissions for that patient are assigned to a single split to prevent data leakage.

Rationale: Temporal splitting prevents a model from "knowing" future outcomes during training—a critical safeguard in time-sensitive clinical prediction. Stratification ensures consistent baseline readmission rates across splits, avoiding confounding due to cohort composition shifts.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Three feature modalities are engineered:

**Clinical notes:** Discharge summaries and clinical documentation are encoded using a frozen pretrained biomedical transformer (BioBERT or SciBERT). This model is pretrained on general biomedical corpora and is *not* fine-tuned on the training labels to avoid encoding label information into embeddings. Each note produces a 768-dimensional contextual vector.

**Imaging:** Echocardiogram images are processed through a frozen pretrained convolutional neural network (DenseNet-121, pretrained on ImageNet). No label-driven fine-tuning is performed. Each image produces a 1024-dimensional feature vector. Image normalization (mean and standard deviation) is computed from training images only and applied identically to validation and test images.

**Structured data:** Age, sex, ejection fraction, BNP level, Charlson comorbidity index components, medication classes (ACE inhibitors, beta-blockers, diuretics, statins), admission source, and length of stay are included. Numerical features are standardized using training set statistics (mean and standard deviation); categorical features are one-hot encoded. All preprocessing transformers are fit on the training set only.

No engineered features are constructed that could encode the readmission label (e.g., post-discharge adverse events or readmission-predictive billing codes are excluded).

### 4. Model Architecture and Baseline

**Multi-modal model:** Three parallel branches are trained:
- Text branch: frozen BioBERT embeddings → bidirectional LSTM (256 units) → attention pooling
- Image branch: frozen DenseNet-121 → global average pooling
- Structured branch: dense layers (128 → 64 units, ReLU)

Outputs are concatenated and passed through a shared dense network (hidden layers: 256 and 128 units, ReLU, 20% dropout) to a sigmoid output. Training uses Adam (learning rate 1e-4), binary cross-entropy loss, and early stopping on validation loss (patience=10 epochs).

**Baseline:** Unimodal model using clinical notes only. Architecture: frozen BioBERT embeddings → bidirectional LSTM → attention pooling → dense layers (128 → 64 units) → sigmoid. Identical optimizer, loss, and early stopping. Both models undergo equivalent hyperparameter tuning (20 random trials, varying LSTM units, learning rate, and dropout). The baseline uses the same pretrained biomedical transformer to ensure feature quality is not a confound.

### 5. Model Selection and Validation

Hyperparameter tuning occurs on the validation set (months 37–48) via random search over 20 trials. The trial achieving the highest validation AUPRC is selected. No further tuning or threshold optimization occurs after model selection. The test set (months 49–60) is held entirely out-of-sample and evaluated exactly once at the end.

### 6. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** Area Under the Precision-Recall Curve (AUPRC). Rationale: readmission is a minority class (~15–25% incidence), making AUROC inappropriate due to high true negative rates. AUPRC directly measures precision (resource efficiency) and recall (patient safety)—both operationally critical to hospital readmission reduction programs.

**Secondary metrics:** Sensitivity at 90% specificity (actionable threshold), Positive Predictive Value (resource planning), AUROC (comparison to literature), and F1-score.

### 7. Confound Controls

Subgroup analysis is performed on the test set to verify that multi-modal gains are genuine and not driven by confounds:
- Demographic stratification: performance by age group, sex, and race/ethnicity
- Comorbidity stratification: performance for Charlson index 0–1, 2–3, 4+
- Treatment stratification: performance stratified by ACE inhibitor and diuretic use
- Temporal stratification: early test window (months 49–54) vs. late (months 55–60) to detect drift
- Documentation completeness: performance for complete vs. partial imaging availability

The hypothesis is supported only if the multi-modal model achieves higher test AUPRC than the baseline *consistently across all subgroups*, indicating that imaging and structured data contribute generalizable signal rather than spurious patterns.
